coredata.dc:title: Anticoagulant and antioxidant properties of hemodialysis membranes modified by hydrogels incorporating carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Teng
Wang, Yi
Wang, Hui
Zhang, Yu
Zhou, Jianhua
Zhao, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Hemodialysis membranes play an important role in the treatment of renal failure, but their anticoagulant and antioxidant properties need to be improved to prevent membrane fouling and oxidative stress in dialysis. To these ends, a heparinoid gel incorporating carbon dots (CDs) was applied to modify polyethersulfone hemodialysis membranes. The CDs were mixed with ammonium persulfate, Sodium p-styrene sulfonate, and N, N - Methylenebisacrylamide monomers, and modified to surfaces of hemodialysis membranes through the in-situ polymerization of these monomers. As prepared hybrid hydrogel featured a high free radical scavenging rate and anticoagulant time for the hemodialysis membrane. The scavenging rates of the modified membrane for 2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2, 2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate (ABTS+) free radicals are as high as 74.01% and 97.78%. The activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), prothrombin time (PT) and thrombin time (TT) of the dialysis membrane are 600 s,283 s and 348 s, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hemodialysis membranes
Anticoagulation
Antioxidant
Biocompatibility

Ultrafiltration coefficient and solute removal rate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors thank final support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22178139), Key R&D Program of Hubei Province (2022BCA079, 2022BCA047), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFC2705103) and Start-up Foundation of Guangzhou National Laboratory (ZL-SRPG22019).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic preparation of CDs, (b–d) TEM image, Lattice fringes, and Size distribution of CDs, respectively, (e–g) UV adsorption, PL excitation, PL emission spectra, fluorescence images of CDs aqueous dispersion (0.1% conc.) under natural light 365 nm and C 1s core level spectra of CDs powder.

(a) Schematic diagram of the modification of PES flat sheet membranes by in in-situ polymerization in the presence of CDs, (b) FTIR spectrum of PES and M-CD membranes, (c) XPS patterns of PES and M-CD membranes.

(a, b) C 1s core level spectra of pristine PES membrane and M-CD1, (c) Optical photographs, (d) microscopic images and (e) fluorescence (irradiated by 543 nm light) pictures of PES and M-CD membranes.

(a) Dynamic water contact angle of M-CD membranes and pristine PES membranes, (b) Picture of changes in membrane water contact angle of M-CD membranes and pristine PES membranes at different times, (c) Effect of operation time in changes the flux of pristine PES membrane and M-CD1, (d) Solute removal rate of creatinine (Cre), urea (Ure) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) of PES pristine membrane and M-CD1.
The (a) surface and (b, c) cross-section morphologies of the pristine PES membrane, The (d) surface and (e, f) cross-section morphologies of the M-CD1 membrane.

(a) APTT, (b) PT and (c) TT values of various membranes (values are expressed as mean ± SD, n = 3, *p < 0.05 compared with the pristine PES membrane), (d) APTT, PT and TT values of 50 μL 0.1 % CDs solution (values are expressed as mean ± SD, n = 3, *p < 0.05 compared with control).

(a) UV–vis spectra of DPPH solutions incubated with various modified membranes, (b) DPPH scavenging ratios of the modified membranes, (c) UV–vis spectra of ABTS+ solutions incubated with M-CD membranes, (d) ABTS+ scavenging ratios of the modified membranes.

(a) UV–vis spectra of DPPH solutions incubated with M-CD membranes that were cleaned in water (100 rpm stirring, 1 h), (b) DPPH scavenging ratios of the cleaned M-CD membranes, (c) UV–vis spectra of ABTS+ solutions incubated with M-CD membranes that were cleaned in water (100 rpm stirring, 1 h), (d) ABTS+ scavenging ratios of the cleaned M-CD membranes.

Generated concentrations of (a) C3a and (b) C5a after various membranes (including pristine and modified membranes) being incubated with whole blood for 3 h. (c) Hemolysis rate of the pristine and modified PES membranes. (d) Inhibiting effect of various membranes (including pristine and modified PES membranes) on lipid peroxidation.

head.ce:title: Anticoagulant and antioxidant properties of hemodialysis membranes modified by hydrogels incorporating carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Material Chemistry for Energy Conversion and Storage, (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Material Chemistry for Energy Conversion and Storage
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Material Chemistry for Energy Conversion and Storage, (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pediatrics, Tongji Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pediatrics, Tongji Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hemodialysis membranes play an important role in the treatment of renal failure, but their anticoagulant and antioxidant properties need to be improved to prevent membrane fouling and oxidative stress in dialysis. To these ends, a heparinoid gel incorporating carbon dots (CDs) was applied to modify polyethersulfone hemodialysis membranes. The CDs were mixed with ammonium persulfate, Sodium p-styrene sulfonate, and N, N - Methylenebisacrylamide monomers, and modified to surfaces of hemodialysis membranes through the in-situ polymerization of these monomers. As prepared hybrid hydrogel featured a high free radical scavenging rate and anticoagulant time for the hemodialysis membrane. The scavenging rates of the modified membrane for 2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2, 2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate (ABTS+) free radicals are as high as 74.01% and 97.78%. The activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), prothrombin time (PT) and thrombin time (TT) of the dialysis membrane are 600 s,283 s and 348 s, respectively.

A new method was introduced to improve both the anti-oxidation and anticoagulation properties of hemodialysis membranes, through the combination of carbon dots and hydrophilic polymers.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: About 850 million people were diagnosed with Chronic Kidney Disease worldwide, among which 4 million patients require regular hemodialysis treatments (2–3 times per week) to sustain their lives [1,2]. In these aspects, hemodialysis has been widely used to replace the function of the kidneys, in which semipermeable polymer membranes are the key components to remove excess toxins in the blood through the dialysis effect while retaining beneficial substances such as proteins in the blood [3]. The global cost of using hemodialysis to treat kidney failure is as high as $66.3 billion per year [4]. Despite significant success, blood hemodialysis membranes face several major challenges. First, the formation of clots and blockage of membrane pores will reduce the clearance rate of the dialyzer during dialysis. Second, oxidative stress during dialysis will increase the risk of diabetes, atherosclerosis and inflammation, and even endanger the patient's life in serious cases [5].

To circumvent the above-mentioned problems, various strategies have been explored to modify hemodialysis membranes. The modification methods for anticoagulation of hemodialysis membranes mainly include blending [6–10] and surface coating [11–15], as well as new crosslinking polymerization methods that emerged in recent years [16–18]. The use of heparin or heparin-like structures (such as molecular structures containing carboxyl or sulfonic groups) to reduce thrombin activity and achieve anticoagulant effects has received increasing attention [19]. Liu et al. synthesized amphiphilic heparin-like polymers and grafted them to the surface of polylactic acid membranes through hydrophobic interactions, which can increase the APTT of the membrane, e.g., from 49 s to over 600 s [20]. At present, the antioxidant modification of hemodialysis membranes mainly includes the following categories: (1): introducing natural antioxidant products [21–25]; (2): molecular loading of antioxidant drugs [26–30]; (3): nanoparticles encapsulation, etc [31–33]. Yang et al. modified silybin by mixing it with a PSF membrane and measured its antioxidant properties based on their DPPH and ABTS+ radical scavenging ratio and lipid peroxidation inhibition. They also reported improvements in clearance and antioxidant properties. In addition, the adhesion of red blood cells and platelets, as well as the levels of superoxide dismutase (SOD), have been reduced, indicating that the membrane prepared above can be suitable for reducing oxidative stress during hemodialysis. However, anticoagulation and antioxidant molecular structure design are difficult to be compatible with each other. Anticoagulant modification is achieved by binding heparin-like functional groups, such as sulfonic and carboxylic groups, to thrombin to inhibit its activity. At the same time, it also enhances the activity of antithrombin, thereby enhancing its anticoagulant effect and ultimately inhibiting the formation of blood clots [34]; Antioxidants can effectively slow down oxidation reactions by eliminating free radicals and clearing singlet oxygen to inhibit the generation of oxidizing substances or promote the conversion of reducing substances. Increasing the proportion of carboxylic acids and neutral hydroxyl groups in the molecular chain of heparin can lead to a decrease in the anticoagulant performance of the polymer.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterial smaller than 10 nm [35,36]. With good water dispersibility, high fluorescence, and nontoxicity, CDs have been used in environmental monitoring [37], chemical analysis [38,39] and more [40–43]. We noticed that CDs contain many hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surfaces, which might confer CDs with antioxidant properties [44]. Moreover, CDs with relatively small sizes (<5.5 nm) can be excreted from the human body through urine [45,46]. These properties render CDs promising potential in the modification of hemodialysis membranes. However, it is very difficult to directly covalently graft CDs onto PES membranes, due to which CDs have not been used to modify hemodialysis membranes by far. In this work, we aim to incorporate CDs during the in-situ polymerization of sodium p-styrene sulfonate monomers on PES hemodialysis membranes. By doing so, we are able to combine the anticoagulant properties of heparin-like polymers with CDs’ antioxidant properties, and thus prepare hemodialysis membranes with anticoagulant and antioxidant properties. For example, the DPPH and ABTS+ free radical scavenging rates of modified membranes are 74.01% and 97.78% respectively, and the APTT performance is above 600 s.

Polyethersulfone (PES) membranes were purchased from RisingSun Membrane Technology (Beijing) Co., Ltd. Sodium p-styrene sulfonate (SSNa), Ammonium persulfate (APS), N, N methylene bisacrylamide (MBA) and 2, 2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (containing 10–20% Benzene) were purchased from Aladdin. Azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonicacidammoniumsalt salt)] (98%) was purchased from Innochem. Fresh human whole blood, poor platelet and platelet-rich plasma were provided by Tongji Medical College of Huazhong University of Science and Technology. The protocol of this study was approved by the Ethical Committee of Tongji Hospital. APTT, PT and PT reagents were purchased from Shanghai Sun Biotech Co. Ltd. C5a and C3a kits were purchased from Wuhan Huamei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Deionized water (<20 μS/cm conductance) is prepared in the lab. Unless otherwise noted, all reagents were used as received from Sigma-Aldrich.

CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method reported elsewhere [47]. 30 g banana was mixed in aqueous ethanol (10 mL, EtOH: H2O volume ratio 3: 2) in a mixer and stirred for 10 min. Then, the mixtures were put into a Teflon-sealed hydrothermal reactor (Wuhan XinShenShi Chemical Technology Co., Ltd., China), sealed, heated at 180 °C for 6 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The product was purified with a Buchner funnel, leaving the lower-layer solution. The reserved solution was further filtrated by a porous membrane (mean pore sizes: 0.22 μm), and centrifuged for 20 min at a speed of 15000 r/min. Then the supernatant was dialyzed using a 500 Da dialysis membrane for 4 days, during which water was changed 3 times a day. The dialyzate was freeze-dried to obtain the CDs powder, which was used to prepare CDs dispersions (10% wt/v).

CDs aqueous dispersion (10 wt%) was added into a flask and diluted to different concentrations with phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4). Then, monomers (SSNa), crosslinkers (MBA) and initiators (APS) were added and homogenized. A piece of flat-sheet PES membrane was fully immersed into the mixture solution prepared above. Oxygen in the flask was removed by 5 times “freezing – evacuating – thawing”, and the flask was kept in an oil bath (70 °C), during which the polymerization occurred on the surface of the flat-sheet PES membrane. Note: The concentration of SSNa, MBA and APS in the reaction mixture were kept at 6 %, 2 %, and 1 %, respectively. Meanwhile, the concentration of CDs was varied (0 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.4 wt%, 0.6 wt%, and 0.8 wt%), and the resultant membranes were named: M-SSNa, M-CD1, M-CD2, M-CD3, and M-CD4, respectively.

Morphologies of the CDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Thermo Scientific, F200X), and the accelerating voltage was 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Thermo Scientific, Nicolet Is50R) spectra of membranes and CDs were recorded by FTIR spectrometer. The wavenumber range is 500–4000 cm−1, the resolution is 4 cm−1, and the scan rate is 16. Membrane morphologies were analyzed by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, HHTNT-536-9424). The water contact angle was measured by a dynamic contact angle meter (Dataphysics, OCA 15 EC). The fluorescence spectrophotometer is used (Cary Eclipse) to measure the fluorescence spectrum of CDs and M-CD membranes. Ultraviolet–visible was recorded with a spectrophotometer (Agilent, Cary60). The fluorescence characteristics of the membranes and CDs were observed with an inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus Bx50). Analyze the fluorescence images of the M-CD membranes and CDs using the upconversion spectral scanning confocal microscopy system (Japan FV1200).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of CDs and modified hemodialysis membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the ability of CDs and membrane to scavenge free radicals and the ability to alleviate oxidative stress under hemodialysis, the scavenging activity of DPPH free radicals and ABTS+ free radicals were carried out according to the literature [30,47].

DPPH (4.06 mg) was dissolved in phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4) (100 mL) to prepare a DPPH solution (conc. 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L). Membrane samples (1 cm2) were randomly selected from the lyophilizer, freeze-dried for 2 h, and added to DPPH solution (3 mL), kept in darkness for 60 min. Then, the membrane sample was removed and the absorbance of the remaining solution at 517 nm was obtained using a UV visible spectrophotometer.
C R
D P P H
was calculated by formula 1.
(1)
C R
D P P H
=
A C
−
A M
A C
× 100
%
above,
A C
is the absorbance of the sample at 517 nm without addition,
A M
is the absorbance of the added membrane at 517 nm.

To evaluate the DPPH scavenging rate stability, M-CD membranes were further cleaned with water (100 ppm stirring, for 1 h). Then DPPH scavenging ratio of the cleaned membranes was measured and calculated by the same method mentioned above.

2.45 mM potassium persulfate solution and 7 mM 2, 2-diazo-di (3-ethyl-benzothiazole-6-sulfonic acid) ammonium salt (ABTS) solution were prepared with absolute phosphate buffer (pH = 7.4). The same volume of potassium persulfate solution was mixed with ABTS solution. After standing in a dark place at room temperature for 12 h to fully react, dilute the absorbance of the solution with absolute ethanol until the ultraviolet absorbance at 734 nm is around 0.70 ± 0.02;

Then, take a membrane sample with a size of 1 cm2 from the freeze-dried membrane at random, immerse it in 4 ml ABTS+ solution, and react in the dark at room temperature for 20 min. After randomly taking a membrane with a size of 1 cm2 from the freeze-dried membrane and immersing the membrane in 4 ml ABTS+ solution, they were sent to a dark place at room temperature for 20 min to react completely and avoid the decrease of concentration, and then test the absorbance at 734 nm with an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer.
C R
A B T S
+
was calculated by formula 2.
(2)
C R
A B T S
+
=
A C
−
A M
A C
× 100
%
among them,
A C
is the absorbance of the sample at 734 nm without addition,
A M
is the absorbance of the added membrane at 734 nm. The absorbance is measured by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer.

To evaluate the ABTS+ scavenging rate stability, M-CD membranes were further cleaned with water (100 ppm stirring, for 1 h). Then ABTS+ scavenging ratio of the cleaned membranes were measured and calculated by the same method mentioned above.
APTT, PT and TT were measured by a coagulation analyzer (Pun-2048B); The test steps are as follows:

After putting 0.1 mL platelet-poor plasma into the cuvette, fold the membrane with the size of 1.0 cm2 and put it at the bottom of the cuvette. Take 0.1 mL APTT reagent and inject it into the cuvette. Preheat at 37 °C for 5 min, balance the CaCl2 with a concentration of 0.025 mol mL−1 at 37 °C for 5 min, take out 0.1 mL of CaCl2 and add it to the blood coagulation cup, and record the time according to the blood coagulation meter.

The degree of complement activation of the membrane before and after modification was measured by the Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay of enzyme-linked immunosorbent. The specific steps were performed according to the instructions provided by Wuhan Huamei Biotechnology Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrafiltration coefficient and solute removal rate
ce:section.ce:para:
The ultrafiltration performance and solute removal rate were studied. The effective area of the membrane is 2.56π cm2, and all membranes have been stabilized (0.08 MPa, 25 °C) to achieve stable performance before testing. Different salts including Lysozyme, creatinine, and Bovine serum albumin were tested at a pressure of 0.08 MPa at 25 °C. Ultrafiltration coefficient (
K u f
) and solute removal rate (R) are determined by the following equation:
(3)
K u f
=
V
A × T
× 100
%
(4)
R =
(
1 −
C p
C f
× 100
%
)
where V is the volume of permeation collected in the operation time T, and A is the effective membrane area.
C p
and
C f
are the solute concentrations of the permeate and feed solution, respectively.

Flatsheet Membranes (1.0 cm2) were pretreated with 37 °C normal saline for 30 min. The whole blood was centrifuged (3000 rpm, 20 min), and the supernatant (200 μl) was injected into the physiological saline after 30 min, and the temperature was also maintained at 37 °C for 120 min. Then a UV visible spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorbance of the remaining solution at 545 nm. The absorbance of pure water and physiological saline at 545 nm was determined at the same time. The hemolysis rate (HR) is calculated by the following formula:
(5)
H R
=
A t
−
A w
A p
−
A w
× 100
%
where A t is the absorbance value of the immersed membrane solution,
A w
is the absorbance value tested with pure water,
A p
is the absorbance value measured with physiological saline.

Wash the surface blood of the pig liver with ice physiological saline, chop the pig liver in an ice water bath, stir for 30 min to break the pig liver, and dilute the pig liver homogenate with physiological saline to a volume fraction of 10%. Use 3000 rpm to obtain the supernatant, and obtain the pig liver tissue homogenate as a lipid source. Dilute the absorbance of the supernatant of pig liver tissue homogenate with PBS solution at 532 nm to 0.65 ± 0.05. After modification, 1 cm2 of membrane sample, H2O2 (0.3 mL, 100 mM), and pig liver tissue homogenate (3 mL, 10 % (v/v)) at 37 °C for 1 h. Take Trichloroacetic acid (3 mL, 15%) to stop the reaction, centrifuge the above-mixed solution at 3000 rpm for 10 min, take the supernatant and add the Barbituric acid (3 mL, 0.67%), heat it in an oil bath at 100 °C for 15 min, cool it with flowing water, and measure the absorbance at 532 nm with an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer.

The inhibition rate (IR) of Lipid peroxidation is calculated by the following formula:
(6)
I R
=
A m
−
A s
A m
−
A n
× 100
%
where
A m
is the absorbance of solution which contained membrane with H2O2,
A s
is the absorbance of control which contained H2O2 without membrane,
A n
is the absorbance of control which contained membrane without H2O2.

Cells suspension (Raw 264.7/HUVEC, 100 μL/well) was seeded in a 96-well plate. The plate was placed in a cell culture incubator for pre-incubation for 24 h. Subsequently, different concentrations of CDs or 1 cm2 membrane were added to the plate, followed by further incubation in the cell culture incubator for 24 h. Then, 10 μL of CCK-8 solution (taking care not to generate bubbles) was added to each well, and the plate was incubated in the cell culture incubator for 2 h. Finally, the absorbance at 450 nm was measured using an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) reader. Calculate as follow:
(7)
C e l l
r e l a v i e
v i a b i l i t y
=
A s
−
A b
A c
−
A b
× 100 %
Where
A s
is the absorbance of the experimental wells,
A c
is the absorbance of the control blank wells, and
A b
is the absorbance of the blank wells.

Fig. 1 a schemes the hydrothermal preparation of CDs. The yield of CDs prepared from bananas is 1.03 wt%. Fig. 1b shows the TEM characterizations of CDs dispersion, in which many dark dots with spherical shapes were seen without aggregation, indicating good water-dispersibility of CDs. From the high-resolution TEM picture of CDs (Fig. 1c), the lattice spacing of CDs is measured to be 0.21 nm, corresponding to the 102 graphene crystal planes [48]. As shown in Fig. 1d, the average size of CDs is 1.5 nm, indicating that these CDs are smaller than 5.5 nm and can be excreted through urination. The synthesis of CDs shows good repeatability (Fig. S1). As seen from Fig. 1e, CDs show maximum UV absorption peaks at 267 nm and 350 nm, which are related to the n - π* electron transition of the CO band and the π - π* electron transition of the CO band, respectively. Meanwhile, CDs show a UV absorption peak at 200 nm, which is related to the n - π * electron transition of –COOH [49]. As shown in Fig. 1f, the optimal fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths of the CDs are located at 460 and 550 nm, respectively, and the emission color fluorescence image of the CDs dispersion under 365 nm excitation is green. The absolute quantum yield of CDs is 3.9%, which was calculated using the integrating sphere of the fluorescence spectrometer FS5. In Fig. 1g, the C1s of CDs can be divided into C–C/CC, C–O, and CO, accounting for 81.07%, 13.18%, and 5.75%, respectively, indicating the presence of carboxyl and carbonyl groups in CDs [50].

Fig. 2 a schemes the in-situ polymerization of SSNa and MBA on surfaces of PES flat-sheet membranes, during which CDs were embedded in the grafted polymers. Compared to the pristine PES membrane (Fig. 2b), both the M-SSNa and M-CD show new peaks around 1020 cm−1, which are assigned to sulfonate acid groups (from SSNa). Compared to M-SSNa, M-CD shows a new peak at 1725 cm−1, which is assigned to carboxylate groups in CDs [51]. Reasonably, the intensity of the 1725 cm−1 peak increases with increasing the CDs amount in M-CD. From XPS characterizations (Fig. 2c), M-SSNa and M-CDs show new peaks for sulfur (106.08 eV) and Na+ (1071.08 eV, from SSNa) [52]. Collectively, these data verify that PSSNa-MBA polymers were grafted on the surface of PES membranes, with CDs embedded in the grafted polymers.

The C 1s core level spectra of PES (Fig. 3 a) shows two different peaks at 284.6 and 286.3 eV, corresponding to CC/C–C and C–O groups, respectively; Fig. 3b is a C 1s core level spectra of modified membrane “M-CD1”. There are three different peaks at 284.6, 286.3 and 287.9 eV, corresponding to CC/C–C, C–O and CO groups, respectively [53]. The C 1s core level spectra of the other modified membranes are shown in Fig. S2. The new XPS peak from the carbonyl group in the CDs indicates the successful introduction of the carbonyl group in the CDs. As shown in Fig. 3c, a yellow color appears with the M-CD membranes and the color turns deeper with increasing the CDs contents in modified membranes. When irradiated by 543 nm light, M-CD membranes show red color as observed by fluorescent microscopy, while the PES pristine membrane remains nonfluorescent (Fig. 3d, 3e) (i.e., black color). This result indicates the incorporation of CDs in the matrix of polymer hydrogel. We also captured images of the modified membrane using laser confocal microscopy (Fig. S3), which also confirms the incorporation of CDs in the membrane.

Hydrophilicity of M-CD membranes was characterized by water contact angle (Fig. 4 a and 4b). The dynamic water contact angle of the pristine PES membrane is stable around 80° (Fig. 4a, square). By contrast, water contact angle of M-CD is significantly lower than PES. Moreover, the equilibrium water contact angle (t = 20 s, indicated by arrow) of M-CD follows the order: M-CD4 (19°) < M-CD3 (20°) < M-CD2 (25°) < M-CD1 (30°). These results indicate that the incorporation of both the SSNa polymers and CDs improves the hydrophilicity of modified membranes. As shown in Fig. 4c, the flux of PES and M-CD1 are 158.6 and 130.6 mL/h−1 mmHg−1, respectively. That is, permeance of the modified membrane is largely (82%) retained compared to the pristine membrane. The solute removal rate of creatinine, urea and bovine serum albumin of PES pristine membrane and M-CD1 is shown Fig. 4d. The retention rate of BSA remains above 99%. The solute removal rates of creatinine and urea small molecule toxins are below 25% and 5%. Thus, small molecule toxins can be cleared smoothly through hemodialysis membranes, while large molecule proteins are preserved.

As shown in Fig. 5 a and d before and after modification, the surface of the membrane is unchanged on the plane. Fig. 5b, c, e and f are the cross sections of the pristine PES membrane and the modified membrane. Fig. 5c and f are enlarged views of Fig. 5b and e. Compared with Fig. 5b and e, there are tiny gel particles embedded on the surface of the membrane, and the particle size is 0.24 μm, it may be that the micro gel partially blocks the membrane pore, resulting in the decrease of the flux.

Anticoagulant property is an important factor in the evaluation of blood compatibility. APTT and PT have been widely used to evaluate coagulation abnormalities in the extrinsic and intrinsic pathways respectively, and TT test is a common method to evaluate the anticoagulant ability of blood contact materials. As seen from Fig. 6 a–c, APTT, PT and TT of M-CD4 are 600 s,348 s and 283 s respectively, which are 12, 6 and 8 times higher than that of the pristine PES membrane without modification. This is consistent with the fact that heparinoid functional groups in the references can effectively extend APTT, PT and TT [20,54,55]. In addition, Fig. 6d shows that the 50 μL 0.1 % solution of CDs was added to plasma for anticoagulant performance testing of APTT, PT, and TT. The results indicate that CDs also have anticoagulant properties. This explains the positive correlation between PT (Fig. 7 b) and TT (Fig. 7c) with the amount of CDs in the modified membranes [56].

DPPH and ABTS+ free radial scavenging rates are tested by UV visible absorption spectra at 517 nm (Figs. 7a) and 734 nm (Fig. 7c), respectively. Then the scavenging rate of DPPH and ABTS+ were calculated on basis of the UV absorption. When the content of CDs in modified membranes increases from 0.2% to 0.8%, the clearance rate of DPPH increases from 42.81% to 74.01% (Fig. 7b). Meanwhile, the clearance rate of ABTS+ increases from 82.69% to 97.48% (Fig. 7d). The improvements in the scavenging rate of both the DPPH and ABTS+ may arise from the excellent anti-oxidant performance of the CDs which contain a lot of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Therefore, the inclusion of CDs greatly enhances the anti-oxidant performance of the PES membrane [57–59].

Then the M-CD membranes were cleaned by water (100 rpm stirring, 1 h), after which their antioxidant performance was tested to study the membrane stability. On basis of the UV absorption data (Fig. 8 a), it is calculated that the scavenging rates of DPPH of cleaned M-CD1, M-CD2, M-CD3 and M-CD4 are 35.41%, 50.83%, 54.87% and 62.17%, respectively. As such, the DPPH scavenging ratio of cleaned membranes was well maintained (>80%) compared with pristine M-CDs membranes without cleaning (Fig. 8b). From the UV–visible adsorption data (Fig. 8c), it is calculated that the ABTS+ scavenging ratios of M-CD1, M-CD2, M-CD3 and M-CD4 are 67.19%, 76.64%, 79.86% and 86.75%, respectively, which are 81.3%, 81.6%, 82.3%, and 88.9% of that of the PES membranes without cleaning (Fig. 8d). Collectively, both the DPPH and ABTS+ scavenging rates of M-CD membranes are stable versus cleaning, which indicates good stability of CDs. In addition, neither the M-CD membranes nor CDs showed cytotoxicity within a wide concentration range of 100–1000 μg/mL (Supplementary Fig. S4).

C3a and C5a, the activation products of complement system, are the common biomarkers for studying the complement activity of biomaterials. From Fig. 9 a and 9b, it can be seen that the C3a and C5a concentrations for the pristine PES membrane are 262 ng/mL and 37 ng/mL, respectively. The C3a and C5a values for various modified M-CD membranes are lower than the above two numbers. The results showed that the PES membrane modified by mixing gel and CDs would not cause significant activation of the complement system similarly. Hemolysis test is usually used to investigate the erythrocyte compatibility of blood-contacting materials. From Fig. 9c, the hemolysis rate of pristine PES membrane is 0.16%, and the hemolysis rates of modified PES with different concentrations of CDs are 0.05%, 0.10%, 0.16%, and 0.32%, respectively. Although the hemolysis rate of modified membranes keeps increasing with the increase of CDs concentration, the highest hemolysis rate of 3.2% is still less than 5%, which meets the safety standards of medical materials [20,60–62]. In addition, we used pig liver as a source of lipids to determine whether modified membranes can inhibit lipid peroxidation. The results are shown in Fig. 9d. The inhibition rate of the pristine PES membrane on lipid peroxidation is 5.02%, which cannot inhibit lipid peroxidation. However, the inhibition rate of M-CD4 on lipid peroxidation reaches 65.15%, which can effectively inhibit lipid peroxidation caused by oxidative stress.

This work aims to improve both the anticoagulation and antioxidant properties of hemodialysis membranes simultaneously. To this end, CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method and explored in conjunction with SSNa, APS and MBA monomers to modify the PES membranes. A microgel incorporated with CDs was grown on surfaces of PES, in which the polymer hydrogel and CDs improved anticoagulant and antioxidant properties, respectively. The DPPH and ABTS+ radical scavenging ratio of the modified membranes can reach 74.01% and 97.78% respectively, while the APTT time is > 600s. This modification effectively improved the biocompatibility of the membrane and provided a new strategy and nanomaterials for hemodialysis to accomplish functional modification. As such, this is an effective method to improve both anticoagulant and antioxidant properties of hemodialysis membranes simultaneously.

Teng Xiao: Data curation, Investigation. Yi Wang: Data curation. Hui Wang: Writing – review & editing. Yu Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jianhua Zhou: Writing – review & editing. Qiang Zhao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests.

ce:section.ce:para:
